data.title: July's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 7.96%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 423 stocks at this time, which is the exact same number listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, there are a couple of names that we'll skip over as they don't quite meet what we are looking for in this screening process. That is The Western Union Company (WU), which hasn't raised its dividend in years. Ennis (EBF) is also a newer name, but similar to WU, they haven't raised their payout in years aside from a large special paid last year. We'll also skip over Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) as they pay a variable dividend.

On top of that, there are several names that we also took a quick look at more recently, so while Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), Flowers Foods (FLO), VICI Properties (VICI) and Lincoln National Corp (LNC) are all interesting names, we'll wait at least a quarter being touching on them again.

#1 RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) 7.96% Yield

RLJ isn't a new name to the list, but it is one that only started screening highly back in November 2024, then has frequently been making the list since. This is a hospitality REIT with 96 hotels in 23 states. RLJ has seen its share price drop just over 20% in the last year, though it is off of the even lower share price levels we saw when we last took a look at the name in April.

Of course, everything dropped sharply in a very volatile April that we experienced when Trump announced retaliatory tariffs, which kicked off a wave of uncertainty. While the broader equity market quickly recovered, there were pockets that had not experienced the same lift. That includes RLJ, which remains down over 30% from its 52-week high.

With remaining depressed, that's continued to keep its dividend yield higher—even if it has come down a touch since our prior update. RLJ is quite sensitive to overall economic conditions, and if economists are expecting higher inflation, pressuring consumers further, then something like traveling for leisure could see a decrease. That could be bad news for a hospitality REIT that counts on visitors to fill their rooms.

They've been raising their dividend aggressively the last few years, but we can see how quickly that can change. During the Covid pandemic, they had nearly eliminated their dividend entirely, keeping just the token penny dividend.

The current payout ratio based on FY 2025 AFFO estimates comes in at a very modest 46%. However, we already saw AFFO decline in FY 2024, and according to estimates, FY 2026 isn't looking too rosy either.

This could be an interesting longer-term play for an investor who doesn't mind the increased risk of investing in a REIT in this space.

#2 United Parcel Service (UPS) 6.22% Yield

Speaking of another area of investing that is being disrupted and providing uncertainty, we have UPS. Naturally, when screening for higher yielders, there tend to be certain industries that tend to provide those higher yields. On the other hand, we have those that make the list due specifically because of broader pressures on their share price. As the share price drops, the yield climbs higher, assuming they don't cut their payout.

UPS has seen its share price drop almost 22% in the last year and is down nearly 29% from its 52-week high. Therefore, we are seeing UPS being in the latter camp of a company that doesn't typically see its dividend yield at these sorts of elevated levels, but the pressures in its industry. UPS has seen its earnings decline significantly for the last two years, and FY 2025 isn't expected to go any differently. That said, analysts are estimating that 2026 could eventually be the turning point where earnings could once again be on the rise.

UPS has raised its dividend for 15 consecutive years, and that includes the last two years, where they kept the streak alive despite the declining earnings. That said, it was the token penny raises that companies sometimes do during these sorts of downturns. In my opinion, keeping the dividend flat or these smaller raises is appropriate during times like this to keep their balance sheet healthier over the longer term.

There was also the massive dividend bump that they had a few years ago, which was after they saw earnings explode higher. Unfortunately, those explosive earnings we saw in FY 2021/2022 didn't last long.

#3 Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) 5.21% Yield

BMY, unlike RLJ and UPS, has actually seen its share price rise by nearly 18% over the last year. However, it is still off its 52-week high by around 26%. Shares participated in the April downturn and have not seen a recovery. In fact, the healthcare sector overall has remained rather pressured and is one of the sectors down YTD, along with consumer discretionary.

Healthcare is often considered quite a defensive area of the market; however, we haven't really been seeing that play out at this time. One of the pressures is a new regulatory environment with RFK Jr really shaking up the space and providing uncertainty.

This isn't the first time we've given BMY a quick look, but it has been quite a while, as you'd have to go back to May 2024 for our last brief update. I generally view the large-cap healthcare companies, such as BMY, as fairly attractive due to their tendency to provide predictable earnings and predictable dividends. BMY has raised its dividend for 17 consecutive years now and has been paying a dividend for 35 years.

Pressuring BMY is that analysts aren't expecting any earnings growth going forward. In 2025, we technically see a massive increase from FY 2024, but there were one-time factors related to their Karuna acquisition and Systlmmune collaboration that pressured 2024 specifically. Using the $7.70 reached in 2022, it has otherwise been a downward trend since that peak.

Looking at FY 2025 estimates, the current dividend works out to a modest payout ratio of less than 37%. That should provide at least some cushion for the company to take its time to potentially return to earnings growth in the future.

At this time, I would view the healthcare space as rather attractive for a long-term investor. I already have a few positions in this space, such as Merck (MRK), AbbVie (ABBV) and Pfizer (PFE), as well as several funds that provide healthcare exposure specifically.

#4 NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE) 5.08% Yield

Similar to healthcare, the utility sector is seen as another area that is traditional defensive with predictable cash flows amid providing necessary services/products. In fact, utilities can be seen as even more predictable, but they can be quite sensitive to interest rate changes. Higher long-term rates make their yields relatively less attractive for income-focused investors, who can sell out of equity utility names and invest more in fixed-income for less risk.

Further, higher short-term and long-term rates can also pressure this space as the costs of borrowing can rise. This is an industry that naturally carries a heavier debt burden given their large infrastructure projects, but also the consistent business means that higher leverage ratios aren't seen as too problematic.

NWE is also not a new name to this list, and we last gave it a look in December 2024. This is a multi-utility company based in South Dakota. They've seen their earnings trending higher over the last couple of years, and analysts expect that to continue for the coming years.

That should bode well for the continued growth in the dividend, as they've been growing their dividend for 19 consecutive years now. Based on FY 2025 estimates, the current dividend payout ratio comes to around 73.7%. For some industries, that could be seen as really high. That is still on the higher end for utilities, but it isn't to an extreme extent or anything.

#5 T. Rowe Price Group (TROW) 5.08% Yield

TROW is a new name that we'll be touching on today, though most investors probably need very little introduction. This is a rather well-known asset manager—it isn't necessarily the largest asset manager, but at ~$1.6 trillion AUM, they are still sizable. Since the FY 2021 EPS peak for the company, they have been moving mostly sideways.

That hasn't kept them from continuing to raise their quarterly dividend, though. They've raised their dividend for 38 consecutive years, and I suspect that is a trend they hope to continue. However, at some point, it would seem that they'll need to find a way to generate some earnings growth rather than this somewhat flat trend.

With a payout ratio of around 61% going forward based on the latest quarterly dividend, they aren't necessarily at any sort of crisis level. However, they are on the higher end relative to their asset manager peers by a fairly high level. BlackRock (BLK), the world's largest asset manager, has a forward payout ratio of ~45% and State Street Corp (STT) is at around 38%, as a couple of examples.

These two larger peers have also shown a trend of generally rising earnings, helping to support dividend growth and some capital appreciation. While the current yield of TROW is quite appealing, I'd find some of its competitors more attractive at this time. STT, in particular, also shows a similar valuation in terms of the forward P/E multiple being around 11x.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Svenska Handelsbanken Q2 Earnings: Not Quite As Bad As It Looks
data.description:
Svenska Handelsbanken's (OTCPK:SVNLF) (OTCPK:SVNLY) second quarter financials were not well received by the market, as shares of the Stockholm-based bank are currently trading around 8% below their pre-results level. That has a driven a roughly similar performance since my last update in May, albeit 2025 has still been a good one for the ADSs, which have returned around 30% so far this year.

Turning back to Q2 results, they don't make for pretty reading at first glance. Revenue, net income and earnings per share were all down double-digits year-on-year, with Handelsbanken clocking in the worst performance of the Nordic banks I track.

While the market's response would suggest otherwise, I don't think last quarter was quite as bad as the headline figures suggest. And with the stock's recent tumble sending its dividend yield over the 12% mark, I think the value case for Handelsbanken remains attractive.

A Poor Set Of Headline Figures

Handelsbanken's headline numbers admittedly look pretty bad, with revenue of SEK 13.6 billion down around 12% year-on-year and 8% sequentially. Earnings also tumbled, with pretax pre-provision income (SEK 7.6 billion), net income (SEK 5.5 billion) and EPS (SEK 2.77; $0.14 per ADS) all down double-digits as well. Per the chart below, the bank's EPS performance was the worst in its peer group:

Net interest income accounts for roughly 75% of Handelsbanken's revenue, the highest mix in its peer set. At SEK 10.7 billion, this line was around 9% lower year-on-year and 6% sequentially, driven by a contracting net interest margin.

Handelsbanken's loan book skews heavily to Swedish real estate lending, particularly residential mortgages. These, in turn, skew more to floating rate than fixed rate. While that was a great when interest rates were rising, it is weighing now that they are declining, with the Riksbank's (Sweden's central bank) policy rate ending the quarter at 2%, down 200bps from last year's peak.

That being said, Handelsbanken's Q2 NII performance still looks poor. Indeed, the Riksbank only cut by 25bps last quarter, and that solitary cut came near the end of the period. In previous quarters, Handelsbanken was able to lower its deposit rates alongside interest rate cuts, dampening the overall impact on its NII. As last quarter was relatively flat from a rate perspective (just one cut right at the end), the bank's asset yields simply caught up a little more on the downswing, resulting in a relatively soft NII print.

Looking at the policy rate forecast above, you can see that this is seen at 1.88% next year, or 12bps below the prevailing rate. Since this is halfway to 25bps, it basically implies around a 50% chance of another rate cut. That means we could be near the end of the easing cycle and a bottom for the bank's NII.

Elsewhere, net fee and commission income was also pretty soft, declining ~2% year-on-year and ~1% sequentially to SEK 2.87 billion. The largest component of this revenue comes from mutual fund fees. At SEK 1.4 billion, this line fell ~7% year-on-year and ~4% quarter-on-quarter.

Management attributed this performance to weak stock markets, which makes sense. Since these fees are booked with a lag, they largely reflect what happened earlier in the year, when there was a large sell-off due to tariff fears. On top of that, Sweden's currency, the krona, also appreciated strongly, which would also have had an impact. The good news is that equity markets have since rebounded strongly, and this should result in stronger mutual fund income in Q3.

Finally, trading income was a significant negative contributor last quarter, declining by over SEK 600 million year-on-year. The good news (if you can call it that) is that this was mainly due to fair value changes in derivatives that Handelsbanken employs to manage various risks. While this can be volatile in any given quarter, it will basically net out to zero over time, as shown below. The important point is that customer-related trading income was stable. Accordingly, I would not extrapolate or read too much into this performance.

Costs were again a positive contributor. As I have mentioned before, Handelsbanken has been undergoing an efficiency drive, as the bank is exiting a period of elevated investment spending in areas like IT. That is allowing it to reduce headcount, particularly among external consultants, aiding its overall cost inflation. With that, total expenses came in at SEK 6.02 billion last quarter, down around 6% year-on-year, helped by lower staffing and IT-related costs.

Credit costs were likewise a positive. The bank booked a release for the sixth straight quarter, with management releasing the last of the macro add-ons it first laid on during COVID. Asset quality remains robust, with Stage 3 loans (non-performing) falling in absolute terms and relative to total balances. Given its prudent underwriting culture and the highly collateralized nature of its loan book (mostly residential and commercial real estate), low impairment charges have long been a feature of Handelsbanken. That said, credit costs will eventually normalize downwards, so this line won't pad earnings forever.

The Valuation

In Stockholm trading, Handelsbanken trades for around SEK 115 as I type (equal to around $5.95 per ADS), down from SEK 128 when I last covered it. That leaves the shares looking pretty cheap. Indeed, while Q2 results may have disappointed the market, the bank has still accrued SEK 7.15 per share in dividends linked to its H1 2025 performance. Annualized, that would put the forward dividend yield at over 12%.

There are a couple of points I would make about this. Firstly, Q2 was not a great one by Handelsbanken's standards, with the bank earning a 12.7% return on equity. That is a little below its long run average, and like I said, at least part of last quarter's performance was due to unrepresentative factors (trading income, for example). Still, annualizing last quarter's EPS would result in a price-to-earnings ratio of 10.4x, which looks quite cheap given it is based on depressed earnings.

The second point I would make concerns the bank's capital cushion. Handelsbanken is still sitting on a comfortable surplus, with its Common Equity Tier 1 ratio clocking in at 18.4% at the end of Q2. That is 50bps, or roughly SEK 4 billion, above the high-end of management's target range, which is further equal to circa ~2% of the bank's current market-cap. At the mid-point, surplus capital would be more like 5% of its current market-cap. Management can use this to support the dividend.

Conclusion

While they clearly disappointed the market, Svenska Handelsbanken's Q2 2025 results weren't quite as bad as they first appear, with an unrepresentative trading result driving a large part of the performance. Down around 8% since my last piece, these shares now offer investors a clear double-digit dividend yield, and that alone can drive an attractive total return. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
